board.verdict:
Where we are: the engine is genuinely strong and under-exposed. We own a 100% offline NLP capture stack (routeVoice + parseVoiceTasks + predictTopic), a live deadline/priority computation (priorities() in TodayWidget), a real expanded-timetable model (CalendarNote.repeat -> noteEvents), and a clean topic/stage/deadline spine. I verified the gaps the team flagged are real, not imagined: BriefingView/TodayWidget only ever render TODAY; CalendarNote (types.ts:282) has NO topicId so a class is a dead block; TopicItem (types.ts:21) has no weight/recurrence/snooze; search.ts is jump-only over budget/inbox/feed/9 settings labels with zero topic coverage and zero actions; cold-start deliberately seeds an EMPTY topic; and the ONLY proactive nudge is the Plus-gated email watcher in alerts.ts. So the diagnosis is consistent: we have a powerful brain wired to a passive, today-only, solo dashboard. The promise is 'your morning in 90 seconds' and we are delivering maybe the first 30 seconds of it.

The hard call I have to make as guardian of the north star: our LOCKED direction (founder offsite, primary user = 22-38 solo knowledge worker; students are wave two; public won't-build = no AI scheduling, no mobile, no team features). The loop is pushing student/freelancer personas and several proposals quietly cross our brand boundary. I am NOT going to chase the freelancer build-out this round (Clients command center, per-client time tracking, client-revenue budget, Freelance onboarding) — that is a second product, not a sharpening of the one knife, and Tom is not who we promised the board we'd serve first. I'm also vetoing the 'auto-plan my day' time-blocking wedge and 'Exam Mode auto-revision-plan': both are AI/auto-scheduling, which is the explicit anti-reference (Sunsama/Akiflow) we said we will NOT become. Bold does not mean off-strategy.

What bold DOES mean this round: extend '90-second morning' into '90-second WEEK', make the capture brain visible and reach the user before they open the app, and turn Ctrl+K into a real command surface. These deepen the existing knife for the existing user. Score 6/10: the foundation deserves a 7-8, but the north star is only half-delivered and the engine is hidden. We are not at sign-off.
topPriorities: P1 — This Week surface (boldness 4, ON north star): add a third Home landing alongside Briefing/Board, a 7-day lane fusing the three sources we ALREADY compute — recurring timetable classes (noteEvents), deadlineEntries, and dailyPlan tasks — each day a column with classes by time, deadline chips on their due day, and free gaps visibly empty. Pure composition over WidgetCtx, mirrors how BriefingView was built. This is the single highest-impact missing piece: it turns 'my morning in 90s' into 'my week in 90s' with no schema/auth/sync risk. Ship this first.

P2 — Proactive local deadline nudges (boldness 5, the actual promise): a FREE, local, deadline-aware notification engine driven off the priorities() computation TodayWidget already does — a morning digest ('3 things today, next class 14:00') and per-deadline nudges, via the Tauri notification plugin already imported in alerts.ts with a web Notification fallback. Today the brief only exists if the user remembers to open Bozz; that silently breaks 'your morning in 90 seconds.' Must stay free and local (no Plus gate, no server) — it is the core promise, not an upsell. No new data model.

P3 — Ctrl+K becomes a command palette that DOES things (boldness 5, for our real 22-38 power user): index topics + topic items into SearchEntry (search.ts indexes neither today) and add an action registry that runs verbs through the engines we already lift in Dashboard — 'add task <text> > Topic', 'deadline <text> friday', 'expense 12 coffee', 'start pomodoro', 'go to <topic>', 'file all predicted'. Everything needed (routeVoice, parseVoiceTasks, predictTopic, addTopicItem/addDeadline/addToInbox, setBudget) already exists and is local. This makes Bozz feel like a pro tool for the solo knowledge worker we promised to serve first, and reinforces 90-seconds by removing the mouse.

P4 (stretch, only if P1-P3 land clean) — Link the timetable to modules (boldness 4): add optional topicId on CalendarNote and auto-link a colour-matched topic per distinct class title, so tapping a class anywhere (NextClassBanner, calendar, the new Week view) opens that topic's links/notes/deadlines. Additive and back-compatible; makes the timetable the spine that connects 'my schedule' to 'my stuff' and gives the P1 Week view real depth. Defer if it threatens TypeScript-green or the P1-P3 timeline.

board.role: CPO (Product) — activation, retention, the core daily loop
board.verdict:
Where we are: five rounds in, the daily-loop SPINE is real and good. The Briefing is the default landing and genuinely delivers "my morning in 90 seconds" (TodayWidget aggregates overdue/today/week priorities + a live next-class banner); typed timetable + offline NL parser + recurring classes are shipped (TypeTimetableForm/timetableParser.ts); the Deadlines hub is first-class (DeadlinesView.tsx); cold-start seeds module-shaped topics; capture is excellent. This is no longer a widget toy — it's a student daily tool. That's why I'm at a 6 not lower.

But the loop STOPS AT TODAY, and three structural gaps are now the ceiling on retention, and I verified all three are still unbuilt:
1. There is no WEEK surface. Maya's mental model is the week; we only ever show today (BriefingView is a single full-width TodayWidget) or a month grid of dots. We answer "my morning" but never "am I behind this week / when am I free." This is the single biggest missing student artefact.
2. Every deadline is EQUAL WEIGHT. TopicItem (types.ts:21) has text/stageId/deadline/dueMin/notes and nothing else — no weight %, no achieved mark. A formative quiz and a 40%-of-module essay look identical. The most emotionally central student job ("am I going to be okay this term") is completely untouched.
3. The typed timetable is a DEAD BLOCK. I confirmed CalendarNote (types.ts:282) has NO topicId — tapping BIO101 anywhere goes nowhere, even though cold-start already creates module topics. The schedule and "my stuff" are siloed.

These are all pure composition / additive-field changes over state we already own (same low-risk pattern as the optional deadline field and homeLanding) — no schema/auth/sync/CI. They are bold AND safe, which is exactly what dogfood v2 wants. I'm deliberately staying on the student persona this round; the freelancer "Clients command center / time-tracking / client-revenue" cluster is genuinely strong but it's a second-persona bet — sequence it AFTER the student week-loop is closed, or we dilute the north star before it's even fully landed.
topPriorities: P1 — Ship a 'This Week' landing (boldness 4): add a third Home landing alongside Briefing/Board, a 7-day (Mon-Fri toggle) lane view fusing the THREE sources we already own — recurring timetable classes (noteEvents), deadline chips on their due day (deadlineEntries), and daily-plan tasks. Each day a column with classes by time, deadline chips, and visibly-empty free gaps so a student SEES when they can work + which days are heavy. Pure composition, mirrors how BriefingView was built; extend homeLanding to 'briefing'|'board'|'week'. Extends the north star from 'my morning' to 'my week in 90 seconds'. THE highest-impact missing piece.

P2 — Module-grade tracking (boldness 5): add optional weight % and optional achieved mark to TopicItem (additive, same risk profile as the optional deadline field). Compute a per-module 'X% secured, Y% remaining, on track for a 2:1' panel on the topic page, and give high-weight deadlines visible HEFT in Deadlines/Today (bigger row + a % badge, sort by weight). Turns Bozz from a to-do list into the tool that answers 'am I going to be okay this term' — the most emotionally central student job and nothing touches it today.

P3 — Link the timetable to modules (boldness 4): add optional topicId to CalendarNote (confirmed absent today) and, in TypeTimetableForm, auto-create/link a colour-matched module topic per distinct class title. Then tapping a class anywhere — NextClassBanner, calendar, the new Week view — opens that module's topic (pinned links, notes, deadlines). Makes the timetable the spine of the whole app and kills the schedule-vs-stuff silo. Additive + back-compatible.

P4 — A daily 'done' moment + a free local deadline notification (boldness 4): (a) add a lightweight today-progress ring to the Briefing (X of Y today-items cleared) with an earned end-state ('Morning sorted — nothing left') and a quiet open-and-clear streak — all computable from prios already in TodayWidget; AND (b) a free, local, deadline-aware morning digest + per-deadline nudge driven off priorities() (reuse the Tauri notification plugin already imported in alerts.ts, web Notification fallback), NOT gated behind Plus. One reframes finishing as a ritual; the other makes Bozz reach OUT so the 90-second morning happens even when she forgets to open the app. Pick whichever the eng round can land cleanly with P1-P3; both are retention engines, not polish.

board.verdict: Craft floor is high and worth protecting. The Briefing (BriefingView.tsx plus TodayWidget.tsx) is our best surface: greeting, narrated summary line, live next-class banner, color-coded priority rows, tasteful empty states. But the whole app's mental model stops at today. Students think in weeks, and no surface answers what is my week. The fallback Calendar defaults to month mode (CalendarView.tsx line 809), a dot grid that says nothing. The typed timetable is a dead block: CalendarNote (types.ts 282-311) has no topicId so a class is untappable and siloed from its module topic. Gorgeous today lens, ugly month lens, missing week lens. Onboarding is still four spotlight walkthroughs of homework before value lands. This round should be bold and structural: extend the 90-second promise from day to week, and make the timetable a living spine. The data already exists in ctx; this is composition and motion work.
topPriorities: Add a This Week home landing as a third option beside briefing and board: a 7-day lane view fusing recurring classes, deadline chips and dailyPlan tasks per day, with free gaps left visibly empty and a per-day load indicator, reusing the Briefing visual language. Highest-impact missing surface; extends the promise to my-week-in-90-seconds.

Make the timetable a living hub: add an optional topicId to CalendarNote (additive, back-compatible) and auto-link a colour-matched module topic per class, so a class becomes tappable from the next-class banner, calendar and week view, opening that module page.

Add optional weight percent and achieved mark to TopicItem (additive). Give high-weight deadlines visible heft with a percent badge and heavier accent, plus a predicted-grade readout on the module page. Most emotionally central student job, untouched today.

Replace the four front-loaded spotlight walkthroughs with a 30-second live setup that materialises the board as the user answers, ending on a populated dashboard; demote the tours to optional. Time-to-first-value is make-or-break.

board.verdict:
Where we are: the engine is genuinely strong and under-exploited. Bozz already owns the two halves no competitor pairs together: a parsed recurring timetable expanded into events (lib/calendar.ts noteEvents, confirmed expanding repeat.weekdays into per-day CalendarEvents) and a live priorities() ranker in TodayWidget.tsx (overdue -> today -> week). The Briefing landing (BriefingView.tsx, homeLanding='briefing' for new accounts) is a real "morning in 90 seconds" surface and it works. TypeScript is green (tsc --noEmit exit 0). But the app is dangerously SPLIT in its identity. The board threw 26 ideas at me across THREE personas (Maya/Priya the student, Tom the freelancer) and the freelancer track is a trap: there is no Freelance cold-start option (COLD_START_OPTIONS in coldStart.ts only has Uni/Job hunt/Gym/Work), Pomodoro persists nothing, Budget is a household ledger, and email has zero topic relationship. Building Clients-command-center + per-client time tracking + invoice aging + client-routed email is a SECOND PRODUCT. Our memory locks the primary user as students/young people and the north star as the student morning. As COO my job is to protect focus: we do NOT chase the freelancer this round. The single biggest GAP is that the brilliant brief only covers TODAY, while a student's actual unit of anxiety is the WEEK and the EXAM. That, plus retention (the brief has no "done" moment, no reason to return) and time-to-value (onboarding is four walkthroughs of homework), are where the leverage is. Everything I'm picking is pure composition over state we already lift, no schema/auth/payments/sync risk.

Satisfaction 6/10: solid foundation, real differentiation in the offline NLP + timetable, but we are one screen short of the core student promise and we have a scope-sprawl problem that will dilute the product if the board keeps voting freelancer.
topPriorities: P1 - SHIP THE WEEK VIEW (the missing core surface). Add a third Home landing 'This Week' alongside Briefing/Board: a Mon-Sun (or Mon-Fri) lane view fusing the three sources we ALREADY compute - noteEvents (timetable classes laid by time), deadlineEntries (deadline chips on their due day), and dailyPlan tasks - with free gaps visibly empty so a student SEES when they can work and which day is overloaded. Extends homeLanding to 'briefing'|'board'|'week' (back-compat: undefined stays 'board'). This is the highest-impact missing piece and turns 'my morning in 90 seconds' into 'my week in 90 seconds'. Pure composition, mirrors how BriefingView was built. Boldness 4.

P2 - MODULE-GRADE TRACKING + EXAM MODE (the emotional core nothing touches). Two halves of the same student-anxiety job, both additive on TopicItem (same risk profile as the existing optional deadline/dueMin fields, no migration): (a) optional weight % and achieved mark on items, with a Grades rollup on the topic page ('Module X: 62% secured, on track for a 2:1') and high-weight deadlines styled with visible heft + a % badge in DeadlinesView/TodayWidget; (b) let any deadline be flagged as an exam/major assessment so the Briefing grows a bold live countdown banner (reuse the exact NextClassBanner 30s re-tick mechanic) - 'STATS EXAM - 4 days'. This is the most emotionally central student job and it's a screenshot-worthy, shareable feature no general productivity app builds. Boldness 5.

P3 - LINK THE TIMETABLE TO MODULES + A DAILY 'DONE' MOMENT (spine + retention). Add optional topicId to CalendarNote and auto-create/link a colour-matched module topic per distinct class title in TypeTimetableForm, so tapping a class anywhere (NextClassBanner, calendar, the new Week View) opens that module's hub with its links/notes/deadlines - removes the silo between 'my schedule' and 'my stuff'. Pair it with a lightweight retention win in the Briefing: a 'today's progress' ring (X of Y today-items cleared), an earned end-state ('Morning sorted. Nothing left today.'), and a quiet daily streak - all computable from prios + dailyPlan already in TodayWidget. This is what makes the brief a daily ritual instead of a worry-list. Boldness 4.

P4 (STRETCH, only if P1-P3 land clean) - ONE-TAP 'PLAN MY DAY' auto-fill: a button that reads today's timed events as busy blocks, finds the gaps, and greedily packs overdue+due-today deadline-bearing tasks (priorities() already ranks them) into PlannerItems coloured by topic. Kills the manual drag ritual in DailyPlannerView and is the wedge against Sunsama/Akiflow - we can auto-block around a real timetable because we parse it and they don't. Reuses PlannerItem/dailyPlan, no schema change. Boldness 5.

opportunities.title: A real Week View: the "what's my week" surface students actually live in
opportunities.rationale: As Maya, my mental model is the WEEK, not the day and not a month grid. But the Briefing (BriefingView.tsx / TodayWidget.tsx) only ever shows today, and Calendar (CalendarView.tsx line 809) defaults to mode='month' — a grid of tiny dots that tells me nothing about where I need to be. There is genuinely no screen that answers "I have 4 classes Mon, Stats essay due Fri 5pm, Thursday afternoon free." BOLD proposal: add a third Home landing alongside Briefing/Board called This Week — a 7-day (or Mon-Fri) lane view that fuses three data sources I already have: recurring timetable classes (noteEvents from timetableParser), deadline entries (deadlineEntries in widgets/util.ts), and daily-plan tasks (dailyPlan). Each day is a column with its classes laid by time, deadline chips on their due day, and free gaps visibly empty so I can SEE when I can work. Pure composition over existing ctx state, no schema/auth/sync changes (mirrors how BriefingView was built). This is the single highest-impact missing piece for a student and directly serves the 'morning in 90 seconds' north star by extending it to 'my week in 90 seconds'.

opportunities.title: Module-grade tracking: weight every deadline and ladder up to a predicted grade
opportunities.rationale: Every deadline in DeadlinesView.tsx and TodayWidget renders as an equal-weight row. But the thing that actually keeps me up at night is 'this essay is 40% of the module.' A formative quiz and a final dissertation look identical right now. BOLD proposal: give topic items (which ARE my modules when seeded from cold-start 'Uni') an optional weight % and an optional achieved mark. Then a Grades surface (or a panel on the module/topic page) computes 'Module X: 62% secured, 38% remaining, on track for a 2:1' and Deadlines sorts/styles high-weight items with visible heft (bigger, a % badge). This is the most emotionally central student job and nothing in the app touches it. It is additive data on TopicItem (no schema migration risk — same pattern as the optional deadline field) and reuses the existing topic/stage model. Turns Bozz from a to-do list into the tool that answers 'am I going to be okay this term.'

opportunities.title: Link the timetable to modules: a class becomes a tappable hub, not a dead event
opportunities.rationale: I typed my timetable and the classes show in Today/Calendar, but CalendarNote (types.ts line 282) has NO topicId — a class is a dead block. When I'm in BIO101 and want the lecture-slides link, the reading deadline, or my notes, I can't get there from the class. Meanwhile cold-start already creates module-shaped topics (coldStart.ts). BOLD proposal: add an optional topicId on CalendarNote and, in TypeTimetableForm, auto-create/link a colour-matched module topic per distinct class title (BIO101, Stats, Lab). Then tapping a class anywhere (Today's NextClassBanner, calendar, the new week view) opens that module's topic page with its pinned links, notes and deadlines. This makes the timetable the spine of the whole app and removes the silo between 'my schedule' and 'my stuff', which is exactly what 'one place for my uni life' should mean. Self-contained React/TS; the linking is additive and back-compatible.

opportunities.title: Auto-plan my day instead of making me drag pills, plus recurring tasks
opportunities.rationale: The Daily Planner (DailyPlannerView.tsx) is a manual ritual — open it, expand each topic dropdown, click day-pills per task. As an impatient first-year I will do this once and never again. And capture has no notion of repeating ('gym mon/wed/fri', 'lab report every week') — extractDeadline in taskParser.ts only ever produces a single startOfDay date, no time-of-day, no recurrence. BOLD proposal two-fold: (1) a one-tap 'Plan my day' that auto-fills today's plan by pulling overdue + due-soon deadline-bearing tasks ranked by urgency (the priorities() logic already exists in TodayWidget) so the plan builds itself; (2) extend the parser + TopicItem to support simple recurrence ('every monday', 'weekly') so chores/recurring coursework regenerate instead of being retyped. Both ride existing data structures (dailyPlan, deadlineEntries) and keep TypeScript green. Removes the biggest 'this is work to use' friction.

opportunities.title: Replace the 4-walkthrough onboarding with a 30-second 'live setup' that builds my board as I answer
opportunities.rationale: Onboarding.tsx greets a brand-new impatient user with a 'Getting started' card saying 'Four short walkthroughs... do them in order' — each a click-by-click spotlight (sidebar, topic page, connect, quicks). That is a LOT of homework before I see value, and the empty Home (HomeView HomeEmptyState) is another wall of choices. As Maya I will bounce or skip all of it. BOLD proposal: collapse the cold-start chip pick (WelcomeColdStart) + typed timetable (WelcomeTimetable) into one continuous 'live setup' where each answer instantly materialises my board behind a translucent panel — pick 'Uni' and a Uni module appears, type two classes and they drop onto a week strip I can see forming. End on a populated dashboard, not a tutorial. Demote the spotlight walkthroughs to an optional 'show me around' for people who want them. The cold-start machinery and timetable parser already exist; this is a flow/composition redesign, not new infra. Time-to-first-value is the make-or-break metric for an impatient student.

opportunities.title: Shared module spaces: see and compare deadlines with coursemates
opportunities.rationale: Everything in Bozz is solo, but real student life runs on 'what's actually due for Stats?' in the group chat and 'can someone send the lab brief?'. The app already has a clean module-topic model and a deadline hub; the natural bold leap is a shareable module. BOLD proposal (frontend-scoped slice): generate a shareable read-only snapshot of a module topic's deadlines + pinned links (an export/import via a copyable encoded blob or file, NOT new backend — staying inside the hard constraints on schema/auth/sync) so a coursemate can import 'Stats deadlines' into their own Bozz in one tap, and a 'compare' view that flags deadlines they have that I'm missing. This seeds virality (every shared module is an invite) and answers the most common student anxiety — 'am I missing a deadline everyone else knows about?'. Implementable purely client-side as serialise/deserialise + a merge-preview UI over existing topic/deadline data.

opportunities.title: Clients command center: one cross-topic board showing all 4 clients at a glance
opportunities.rationale: Bozz models everything as 'topics' (Topic in src/lib/types.ts; rendered one-at-a-time via TopicView.tsx, reached only through the sidebar nav built in Dashboard.tsx navItems). The only cross-topic surfaces are the Today brief (TodayWidget.tsx) and the Deadlines hub (DeadlinesView.tsx) — both flatten everything into a deadline list. As Tom with 4 clients I never get the one view I actually want: a side-by-side status board where each client is a card showing its current pipeline stage counts (SummaryWidget.tsx already computes doing/todo/done but ONLY globally), next deadline, last activity, and who's waiting on whom. Build a 'Clients' (cross-topic) section that renders one card per topic: stage breakdown, next due item from deadlineEntries, an 'unread email count' if a client email is matched, and a 'waiting on you / waiting on them' flag derived from a designated stage. This is pure composition over existing topic/stage/deadline state — no schema, auth, or sync changes — and turns the sidebar-hopping into a true 90-second 'state of my business' glance. Bold, on-north-star, and high-leverage for the freelancer persona the app currently only pays lip service to.

opportunities.title: Per-client time tracking baked into Pomodoro + a billable-hours rollup
opportunities.rationale: PomodoroWidget.tsx is a pure countdown: it tracks 'completed' count in local component state and throws it away (nothing persisted, no topic link, no log). Freelancers literally sell time, so a timer that doesn't record what you worked on or for whom is a glaring miss. Redesign the timer into a client-aware time tracker: a start/stop stopwatch (and Pomodoro mode) that attaches each session to the current topic (ctx.currentTopicId is already available inside TopicView's instanceCtx) plus an optional task, persists sessions to a new local 'timeSessions' state key (same save() pattern as habits/dailyPlan in Dashboard.tsx), and rolls them up into a per-client 'hours this week' figure on the client card and topic page. With an hourly-rate field per topic this also yields an instant 'unbilled value' number. All frontend, self-contained, and it converts the most-used focus widget into the freelancer's billing backbone.

opportunities.title: Turn the budget into client-revenue + unpaid-invoice tracking instead of one household ledger
opportunities.rationale: BudgetData (src/lib/types.ts) is a single flat ledger: recurring items, one-off transactions, savings goals, and an 'owed-to-me' transaction type (iouTotals in lib/budget.ts) that nobody ties to a client. BudgetWidget.tsx shows only 'this month net' + top savings goal — framing built for a student's allowance, not a freelancer's cash flow. As Tom I need: revenue per client, which invoices are outstanding (an 'owed-to-me' amount linked to a topicId), and an aging view ('Acme owes you 1,200, 28 days overdue'). Extend transactions with an optional clientTopicId and a 'paid/unpaid' flag, add an 'Outstanding' rollup grouped by client to BudgetView, and surface 'unpaid: X across N clients' on the client cards. Reuses the existing transaction array and money-formatting helpers; no payment/OAuth/Supabase-schema work. This is the single biggest reason a freelancer would keep Bozz open vs a student to-do app.

opportunities.title: Client-routed email: auto-group the inbox by client and badge each client card
opportunities.rationale: Email is a generic merged inbox (EmailsWidget.tsx 'top 2 unread by importance'; EmailView.tsx; sync in lib/email.ts) with zero relationship to topics. The app already has the routing primitive (predictTopic in lib/taskParser.ts matches text to a topic by keywords/name) and topics already carry keywords. For someone living in browser tabs across 4 clients, the killer feature is 'show me only Acme's threads' and 'Acme has 3 unread' on their card. Add per-topic sender/domain matching (a topic.emailDomains field, or reuse keywords) and: (1) a 'Client emails' filter in EmailView, (2) an unread-by-client badge fed into the Clients command center cards, (3) a recent-emails widget variant scoped to ctx.currentTopicId on the topic page. The Job Hunt world already wires recentEmails onto a topic page (worlds.ts) — generalize that into real client-scoped mail. Frontend-only, leverages existing email cache + prediction.

opportunities.title: A real Freelancer onboarding path + client pipeline template (not student framing)
opportunities.rationale: The freelancer is an afterthought in onboarding: COLD_START_OPTIONS (lib/coldStart.ts) has no 'Freelance/Clients' choice — the closest, 'Work', drops the Deep Work world (a timer + task list) and lists 'client' only as a routing keyword; the 'Freelancer' starter (registry.tsx STARTER_TEMPLATES) is merely today+budget+clock+quickAdd with no client structure. There is no client-pipeline World, while students get Study and Job Hunt with bespoke stages. Add a 'Freelance' cold-start option and a 'Client' template World (worlds.ts) with freelancer-native stages (e.g. Lead / Proposal / In progress / Delivered / Invoiced / Paid — note the existing Job Hunt world already proves bespoke stages + a done stage work) and a topic page pre-arranged with the time tracker, client emails, deadlines, links (brief/Figma/repo) and a pinned note. Also let a new freelancer create one topic PER client in a 'Clients' folder in one tap. This makes the very first run land Tom on a client-shaped dashboard instead of a classroom one.

opportunities.title: Context-aware quick capture that logs against the client you're viewing
opportunities.rationale: Capture is topic-blind: QuickCapture.tsx and the sidebar voice handler route via predictTopic/routeVoice (lib/voiceRouter.ts), and addToInbox/addDeadline in Dashboard.tsx guess a destination from text, but none of them use the client page you're currently on. As Tom, when I'm staring at the Acme page and think 'remind client about feedback', I want it filed to Acme instantly — not dumped in Quicks to triage later or mis-predicted to another topic. Make capture context-aware: when ctx.currentTopicId is set (TopicView already passes it), default the capture destination to that client with a one-tap 'file here' affordance, and add a global 'log time / add note to <current client>' action. Small, self-contained change on top of the existing capture + prediction plumbing, but it removes the constant triage tax that a tab-juggling freelancer feels on every single capture.

opportunities.title: Deadline reminders that reach Priya before she opens the app
opportunities.area: Notifications / core loop (lib/alerts.ts, TodayWidget.tsx, addDeadline in Dashboard.tsx)
opportunities.rationale: The north star is 'your morning in 90 seconds,' but today the brief only exists if Priya remembers to open Bozz. The ONLY proactive notification path (lib/alerts.ts) watches incoming email by keyword and is gated behind Bozz Plus. A captured deadline like 'stats essay friday 5pm' never pings her. For a non-techy 19-year-old who 'stalls without being told the next step,' a silent app that waits to be opened is the single biggest failure of the promise. BOLD move: build a free, local, deadline-aware notification engine (reusing the existing Tauri notification plugin already imported in alerts.ts, plus the web Notification API as fallback) that fires a morning digest ('3 things today, next class 14:00') and per-deadline nudges ('Stats essay due in 2 hours'). Drive it off the priorities() computation TodayWidget already does, so no new data model is needed. This turns Bozz from a dashboard you visit into an assistant that reaches out, which is what actually delivers the 90-second-morning to someone who would otherwise forget the app exists.

opportunities.title: A daily 'done' moment: celebrate finishing the morning, not just listing it
opportunities.rationale: The morning brief (TodayWidget) is purely informational: it lists overdue/today/this-week items and lets you advance a stage, but completing your last task produces nothing. There is no 'you're clear for the day,' no progress bar, no streak. Streaks exist ONLY in the separate, opt-in Habits widget, completely divorced from the core deadline loop. A 19-year-old needs a reason to come back daily and a dopamine hit for clearing the list; right now finishing feels identical to a half-empty list. BOLD: add a lightweight 'today's progress' ring to the Briefing (X of Y today-items cleared) plus an earned end-state ('Morning sorted. Nothing left today.') and a quiet daily-streak counter for days where the brief was opened and cleared. All computable from prios + dailyPlan already in TodayWidget. This reframes the brief from a worry-list into a satisfying daily ritual, the kind of habit loop that keeps a casual student user retained.

opportunities.title: A persistent, always-visible 'next step' guide that replaces front-loaded tours
opportunities.area: Onboarding / activation (HomeCoachChip.tsx, Onboarding.tsx, finishWelcome in Dashboard.tsx)
opportunities.rationale: Priya is explicitly the user who stalls without being told the next step, yet Bozz front-loads everything into welcome overlays and then largely lets go: finishWelcome() dismisses all four spotlight walkthroughs by default, and the only ongoing guidance is HomeCoachChip, a single tiny dismissible pill that hides for the whole day once tapped or X'd and only shows one suggestion at a time. The four named walkthroughs are powerful (they spotlight exact controls and auto-advance) but a non-techy user has to know to go to Settings and 'Replay walkthroughs' to ever find them again. BOLD redesign: turn activation into a persistent, friendly 'Set up Bozz' checklist that lives on Home until complete (add timetable, capture your first thing, build a topic, connect inbox), each row launching the matching existing walkthrough flow, with visible progress. This keeps the existing spotlight engine but makes the next step always discoverable instead of a one-shot overlay she'll skip and never see again.

opportunities.title: Make capture intelligence visible: teach Priya she can just type a sentence
opportunities.area: Capture / quick-add discoverability (QuickAddModal.tsx, taskParser.ts, BriefingView BriefingDeadlineBar)
opportunities.rationale: Bozz has genuinely impressive offline capture: taskParser.ts splits 'call Adrien and buy strings,' extracts dates from 'friday 5pm,' and predictTopic routes it to the right topic, all with no API cost. But this magic is nearly invisible to a non-techy user. The Briefing deadline bar placeholder ('stats essay friday 5pm') is the only hint; the QuickAddModal leads with a generic 'what's on your mind?' and the mic. Priya won't discover that natural-language dates and auto-routing exist, so she'll either over-structure (manually picking topic + date every time) or not capture at all. BOLD: make the parser's understanding a first-class, demonstrated feature, showing the detected date and predicted topic as live confirmable chips AS she types in quick-add (the data is already produced by parseVoiceTasks on every partial), plus a tiny 'try typing: gym tomorrow 6pm' rotating example. Selling and surfacing the smart-capture is a differentiator that makes the app feel like it understands her, and it directly lowers the effort of the core capture action.

opportunities.title: One-tap weekly view of what's coming, framed for a student's real anxiety
opportunities.area: New flow / planning surface (TodayWidget priorities, DeadlinesView.tsx, upcomingEvents in Dashboard.tsx)
opportunities.rationale: Today's brief answers 'what's my morning,' but a student's actual recurring anxiety is 'what's my week, am I behind on anything.' The data exists (upcomingEvents over 14 days, deadlineWindowEvents over 120 days, the Deadlines hub) but it's split across a separate Deadlines section Priya has to navigate to, and the morning brief caps at 5 priorities with a '+N more' that just routes away. There's no single calm 'here's your week' artefact she can glance at on a Sunday night. BOLD: add a 'This week' lens, a horizontal Mon-Sun strip on Home showing classes + deadlines per day with load indicators (busy days flagged), so a non-techy user instantly sees 'Thursday is packed, essay due Friday' without reading a list. It reuses upcomingEvents/noteEvents already computed in Dashboard. This is the kind of ambient, zero-effort foresight that makes Bozz feel like it's looking after her week, not just her next two hours.

opportunities.title: Cold-start that seeds a usable board even when she skips the timetable
opportunities.rationale: The welcome flow's payoff is almost entirely contingent on Priya producing a timetable. coldStart.ts deliberately seeds NO tasks ('we don't fabricate fake deadlines anymore') and the topic starts empty; if she taps 'Skip for now' on the timetable step (very likely for a non-techy user who doesn't have an .ics link handy and finds typing classes tedious at minute one), she lands on a Briefing that says 'nothing pressing' and a topic with nothing in it. The activation moment evaporates. BOLD: make the post-welcome state self-evidently valuable even with zero input, by having the cold-start choice plant a few real, editable starter prompts tailored to the chosen space (e.g. Uni: 'Add your next assignment', 'When's your first exam?') rendered as tappable cards in the brief that each open the smart-capture pre-filled, so the empty state becomes a guided fill-in rather than a void. This bridges the gap between 'I picked Uni' and 'my dashboard does something,' which is exactly where a stall-prone user drops off.

opportunities.title: Turn Ctrl+K into a true command palette that DOES things, not just jumps
opportunities.rationale: Today Ctrl/Cmd+K opens SearchModal, which is jump-only: buildSearchIndex (src/lib/search.ts) indexes ONLY budget transactions, inbox items, calendar feed events, and a hardcoded list of 9 settings labels, and onChoose can only call onJump(section) (SearchModal.tsx line 41-45, wired at Dashboard.tsx:1894). It does not index topics, topic tasks, or notes, and it cannot execute a single action. For a power-user this is the biggest miss in the app. Bold redesign: make it a Raycast/Linear-style command palette where typing runs verbs against the whole app — 'add task <text> > Topic', 'expense 12 coffee', 'start pomodoro', 'new topic', 'go to <topic>', 'file all predicted', 'advance <task>', 'deadline <text> friday'. All the engines already exist and are pure/local: routeVoice + parseVoiceTasks + predictTopic (src/lib/voiceRouter.ts, taskParser.ts), addTopicItem/addToInbox/addDeadline/onAdvanceStage and setBudget are all already lifted in Dashboard.tsx. The palette just needs an action registry plus to also index topics + topic items into SearchEntry. This is the single highest-leverage change for keyboard-first speed and would make Bozz feel like a pro tool instead of a dashboard.

opportunities.title: Keyboard-driven Quicks triage queue (j/k + number-to-file + Accept-row)
opportunities.rationale: Capture is excellent (voice ramble, live parsing, predicted topics) but triage is the bottleneck. In InboxView.tsx each row is mouse-first: you click a ChoicePicker dropdown to pick a topic and click 'send'. The only keyboard affordance is Enter-on-row-to-file (InboxView.tsx:62-70) and one bulk 'Accept all predicted' button. A power-user with 20 quicks wants to blow through them: focus the queue, j/k to move between items, press 1-9 to assign the matching topic (the dests array is already ordered and finite), 'd' to set 'today' deadline, 'x' to delete, Enter to accept the predicted topic, 'u' to undo. Predictions already exist per-row (predictTopic re-run live). This converts a slow, click-heavy chore into a 5-second keyboard sweep and directly serves the 'triage later, fast' promise of Quicks.

opportunities.title: Recurring tasks + reusable task templates for repeating life
opportunities.rationale: TopicItem (src/lib/types) only carries text/stageId/deadline/dueMin/completedAt — there is no recurrence, no snooze/defer, and no templates, even though BudgetData already has a full recurring model (Dashboard.tsx:218-221 migrates recurring frequency/dayOfWeek). A student/power-user has loads of repeating obligations (weekly problem sets, 'water plants every Tuesday', monthly rent reminder, gym 3x/week). Today they must re-type each one. Bold add: give tasks/deadlines an optional recurrence (daily/weekly/monthly + dayOfWeek, mirroring the budget model) so completing one spawns the next, plus a small 'Templates' concept — save a frequently-typed task (or a set, like 'new module checklist') and stamp it into any topic. The capture parser (taskParser.ts) could even detect 'every monday'/'weekly' phrases the way it already detects deadlines. This is real automation, not polish, and it compounds for a daily user.

opportunities.title: Snooze / defer and a unified cross-topic task view with bulk actions
opportunities.rationale: Tasks live siloed inside each topic's TopicTodosWidget; the only cross-cutting views are read-mostly (TodayWidget brief, Deadlines hub, upcomingDeadlines widget). There is no single place to triage ALL open tasks across topics with multi-select bulk actions (reschedule 8 things to next week, advance a batch of stages, bulk-delete), and no snooze/defer to push a task out of sight until a date. For a power-user juggling many topics this forces topic-hopping. Bold: a dedicated keyboard-navigable 'Tasks' workspace that flattens every topic's items with filters (overdue/today/week/no-date), shift-click / space multi-select, and bulk reschedule/advance/snooze — reusing the existing addTopicItem/onAdvanceStage/updateItem plumbing. Snooze just needs a 'snoozedUntil' field on TopicItem honored by the brief/deadline queries (todayEvents/upcomingEvents in Dashboard.tsx). High density + batch control is exactly what this persona wants.

opportunities.title: Customizable global hotkey map with nav chords and per-item shortcuts
opportunities.rationale: The entire global keyboard surface is two bindings hardcoded in one effect in Dashboard.tsx:660-676 (Ctrl/Cmd+K = search, Ctrl/Cmd+B = quicks). There are no section-nav hotkeys, no chord support (Linear-style 'g h' home, 'g c' calendar), no '?' to show a shortcut cheatsheet, and no per-widget hotkeys (e.g. start/stop the Pomodoro, focus the topic add-item input). Bold but contained: introduce a tiny central hotkey registry/dispatcher (single keydown listener, scope-aware, with a chord buffer) plus a '?'-triggered overlay listing every shortcut, and let power-users see/learn them. This is self-contained React/TS, touches no auth/payments/sync, and turns Bozz into something a power-user can drive almost entirely from the keyboard — reinforcing 'your morning in 90 seconds' by removing the mouse from the loop.

opportunities.title: A dense, no-edit-mode 'cockpit' Home layout option
opportunities.rationale: Home is either the zero-config Briefing (single full-width TodayWidget, max-width 760px — BriefingView.tsx:173-183, deliberately roomy and single-column) or the Board, which is react-grid-layout and requires entering Edit mode to change anything (HomeView/TopicView pattern). There is no high-density 'everything at a glance' mode: no compact/dense toggle, no multi-column information-dense default for someone who wants maximum signal per pixel. Bold: add a third Home mode — a fixed, opinionated 'Cockpit' that packs today's tasks, deadlines, next event, habits, pomodoro and budget net into a tight multi-pane grid with smaller type and no drag chrome, optimized for power-users who want density over customization. Reuses every existing widget; it is purely a new composition + an appearance.homeLanding value ('briefing' | 'board' | 'cockpit'), so it is low-risk and self-contained while delivering the density this persona explicitly wants.

opportunities.title: Auto-Plan My Day: one-tap time-blocking that drops your due tasks into the gaps between your real classes
opportunities.area: Daily planner + Today brief (lib/calendar.ts, DailyPlannerView.tsx, PlannerItem type, TodayWidget.tsx)
opportunities.rationale: This is the single boldest wedge against Sunsama and Akiflow, whose entire $20/mo pitch is 'time-block your day' — but they make you drag every block by hand and have NO idea what your timetable looks like. Bozz uniquely already owns BOTH halves of the equation and just isn't connecting them: it expands the student's real recurring timetable into CalendarEvents (timetableParser -> CalendarNote.repeat -> noteEvents in lib/calendar.ts), it already computes today's prioritised due tasks (priorities() in TodayWidget.tsx ranks overdue/today/week), and it already has a PlannerItem grid type (date/startMin/duration/topicId) plus a DailyPlan store. Ship an 'Auto-plan my day' button that reads today's timed events as busy blocks, finds the free gaps, and greedily packs the overdue+due-today tasks into them as PlannerItems coloured by topic — defaulting to ~45min blocks (or the Pomodoro length). No competitor can auto-block around a student's lectures because no competitor parses a typed timetable. It turns the manual DailyPlannerView drag ritual into a literal 'my morning is planned in 90 seconds' moment, which is the exact north star. Fully offline, pure scheduling math over data that already exists in WidgetCtx, no schema change beyond reusing PlannerItem.

opportunities.title: Focus that counts: bind the Pomodoro to a task + topic and feed real study-hours into Review and habit streaks
opportunities.area: PomodoroWidget.tsx, ReviewView.tsx/review.ts, Topic/TopicItem types, new lightweight focus log
opportunities.rationale: Right now the Pomodoro is a throwaway egg-timer — completed sessions live in component state and vanish on unmount ('X pomodoros this session'), linked to nothing. Every serious student tool (Forest, Notion's pomodoro plugins, study planners) makes focus MEASURED and REWARDED; Bozz throws it all away. Make the timer pick a topic/task before starting (the topic list is already in WidgetCtx), persist each finished focus block to storage (date, topicId, minutes — mirrors the Habit.entries Record pattern), then surface 'X hours focused, top subject: Stats' in the weekly Review (which today only shows spend + raw topic counts) and optionally count a daily focus goal as a habit streak. This converts a generic widget into a defensible 'study analytics' feature students screenshot and share — and it makes the Review ritual (Bozz's Things/Sunsama-shutdown answer) actually feel earned instead of just a spend table. Self-contained: one new persisted array + reads in two existing components.

opportunities.title: Exam Mode: a deadline becomes a live countdown + auto-built revision plan that takes over the brief
opportunities.area: TodayWidget.tsx (priorities/NextClassBanner pattern), DeadlinesView.tsx, taskParser deadline extraction, TopicItem
opportunities.rationale: Student planners win on ONE emotional thing Notion/Sunsama/Things completely ignore: the looming exam. Bozz already extracts deadlines from natural language (taskParser.extractDeadline) and already renders a live, self-refreshing 'Next: BIO101 · in 18 min' banner (NextClassBanner re-ticks every 30s). Reuse that exact mechanic for the thing students actually panic about: let any deadline be flagged as an exam/major assessment, and when one is within N days the Briefing grows a bold 'STATS EXAM · 4 days · 9 revision sessions left' banner with a countdown, plus an auto-generated backwards revision plan (split the days-remaining into focus blocks via the auto-plan engine above). This is a signature, screenshot-worthy student feature that maps perfectly onto Bozz's 'real signals, zero config' identity and existing live-banner + deadline code, while being something no general productivity app would ever build.

opportunities.title: Type-anything universal capture bar that parses task + date + topic + money inline, everywhere
opportunities.rationale: Bozz's quiet superpower is a 100% offline NLP stack that competitors PAY OpenAI for: routeVoice classifies money vs topic vs inbox, parseVoiceTasks splits multi-task rambles, predicts topic, and extracts deadlines — all with no API key. But today this intelligence is mostly reachable only via the mic; the typed BriefingDeadlineBar deliberately throws away routing ('parse with no topics so we only lift text + date'). Promote it to a single Cmd/Ctrl-K-style omni-capture bar pinned across the app that runs the FULL routeVoice+parseVoiceTasks pipeline on typed text: 'spent £6 on coffee' -> budget, 'stats essay friday 5pm' -> dated task auto-filed to Uni, 'call Adrien and buy strings' -> two tasks. Show the live predicted destination chip (the InboxView 'predicted' Sparkles badge already exists) and let Enter commit. Akiflow's headline feature is exactly this command bar — and they need an LLM and a login for it. Bozz can ship it offline, instant, and free. Pure reuse of two existing libs wired into one new always-present input.

opportunities.title: Shareable World templates: let students export/import a topic-page World as a code so a class can swap a ready-made 'CS Year 2' dashboard
opportunities.rationale: Notion's entire growth flywheel is community templates; Bozz already has the superior primitive — a BozzWorld is a self-contained, serialisable bundle of look + stages + widget layout (worldToTopic builds a whole pipeline-and-widgets topic from one object) and the apply machinery is appearance/layout-only so it never touches user data. Today Worlds are a closed Plus catalogue. Make a World user-generatable and shareable: 'Save this topic page as a World' serialises the current topic's color/icon/stages/widgetLayout into the existing BozzWorld JSON, encodes it to a short share string (or a copyable blob), and 'Apply from code' deserialises it through the existing canApply/worldToTopic path. One student builds the perfect 'Dissertation' or 'Med School rotations' board and the whole cohort installs it in a tap. This turns a paid cosmetic feature into a viral student-to-student distribution channel with almost no new code — it's serialisation around primitives that already exist — and it's a moat Notion-template-marketplaces can't match because Bozz Worlds carry working widgets + pipelines, not just static pages.

opportunities.title: Smarter-than-it-should-be capture: learn from triage corrections so prediction adapts to each student's vocabulary
opportunities.area: taskParser.predictTopic/topicScore, voiceRouter scoring, InboxView triage (onAssign), Topic.keywords
opportunities.rationale: Bozz already predicts a destination topic for every captured item and offers 'Accept all predicted', but the model is static keyword scoring — when a user overrides the prediction in Quicks (picks a different topic than the Sparkles badge suggested), that correction is thrown away. Capture it: when a user files an item to a topic OTHER than the predicted one, harvest the item's salient words and quietly append them to that topic's keywords array (which topicScore already weights as the STRONGEST signal at +6). Over a week, prediction silently tunes to a given student's slang ('phys' -> Physics, 'sesh' -> Gym) with zero ML, zero network, zero settings. This is the kind of 'it just gets me' magic that makes a planner sticky and is the natural, ambitious payoff of the offline-NLP investment Bozz has already made — a learning loop competitors with cloud LLMs would frame as a premium feature, shipped here as a free, private, local side-effect of normal triage.

priorities.title: Ship the 'This Week' landing — extend the 90-second morning to the 90-second week
priorities.why: Unanimous P1 from all five board members and the single highest-conviction bet in the room. The app's mental model dead-ends at TODAY: BriefingView (BriefingView.tsx) is just one full-width TodayWidget, and the calendar fallback defaults to a month dot-grid. A student's actual unit of anxiety is the week ('am I behind / when am I free'), and we already compute every input it needs: noteEvents() (calendar.ts:68) expands recurring timetable classes across a multi-day window, priorities()/deadlineEntries (TodayWidget.tsx:70) give deadline chips on their due day, and dailyPlan gives planned tasks. Build a third homeLanding='week' (back-compat: undefined stays 'board', mirrors how 'briefing' was added at types.ts:404 and consumed at Dashboard.tsx:707) — a 7-day Mon-Fri-toggle lane, classes laid by time, deadline chips on their day, free gaps left visibly empty, plus a per-day load indicator. Pure composition over WidgetCtx, exactly the low-risk pattern BriefingView already proved. Closes the biggest missing core surface with zero schema/auth/sync risk.

priorities.title: Module-grade tracking — add optional weight % + achieved mark to TopicItem and answer 'am I going to be okay this term'
priorities.why: The boldest bet on the table (boldness 5) and the most emotionally central student job, which literally nothing touches today. Verified: TopicItem (types.ts:21-35) is text/stageId/completedAt/deadline/dueMin/notes — every deadline is equal weight, so a formative quiz and a 40%-of-module essay render identically. Add optional weight and achieved fields (additive, the SAME risk profile as the existing optional dueMin field — no migration, TypeScript stays green). Then compute a per-module rollup on the topic page ('62% secured, on track for a 2:1') and give high-weight deadlines visible heft — a % badge and heavier accent — in DeadlinesView and the TodayWidget priority rows (the priorities() ranker at TodayWidget.tsx:70 already has the items in hand to sort by weight). CPO, Design and COO all rate this their highest-boldness item; the CEO only deferred it on sequencing, not value. It converts Bozz from a to-do list into the tool that answers the term-survival question — a screenshot-worthy feature no general productivity app builds, and it deepens the existing knife for the existing user rather than chasing a second persona.

priorities.title: Free, local, deadline-aware notifications — make the 90-second morning reach the user before they open the app
priorities.why: This is the literal north-star promise, flagged by CEO, CPO and COO. Today the morning brief only exists if the user remembers to open Bozz, which silently breaks 'your morning in 90 seconds.' Drive a morning digest ('3 things today, next class 14:00') and per-deadline nudges directly off the priorities() computation TodayWidget already does (TodayWidget.tsx:70) — no new data model. The plumbing exists: alerts.ts already imports the Tauri notification plugin (@tauri-apps/plugin-notification, alerts.ts:103/123) and runs a background poll loop; we add a deadline-driven check alongside the email watcher, with a web Notification fallback for the PWA. Critically this MUST stay free and local (no Plus gate, no server) — the email watcher is the Plus upsell, but the deadline nudge is the core promise itself, not a feature to sell. Chosen over the Ctrl+K command palette and the timetable-to-module link (both strong, both lower board convergence) because it is the one item that makes the product deliver its stated promise even on the days the user forgets to launch it.

experiments.priority: Ship the 'This Week' landing — extend the 90-second morning to the 90-second week

variants.name: Variant A — The Week Lane (timeline grid)
variants.approach: The literal read of the brief and the safest bet: a 7-day (Mon-Sun) horizontal lane of day columns, with a Mon-Fri / 7-day toggle. Each column is a vertical time axis (e.g. 08:00-20:00) onto which timetable classes from noteEvents(calendarNotes, now) are laid as positioned blocks by startMin/endMin (height = duration, top = start), deadline chips from priorities()/deadlineEntries() pinned as a small badge row at the top of their due day, and free gaps left visibly empty so 'when am I free' is answered by literal whitespace. A slim per-day load bar under each column header (sum of busy minutes + deadline weight, normalised across the week, colour-ramped quiet->packed) answers 'am I behind / which day is brutal' at a glance. TODAY's column gets a highlighted border + a live 'now' line (a thin rule positioned at current time, the only stateful bit, ticking on a 60s interval like NextClassBanner already does). Implementation: new WeekView.tsx is a pure layout component; a buildWeek(ctx, now) selector in a small helper (or inline useMemo) produces, per day, {date, timed: eventsOnDay(allEvents,d).filter(!allDay), allDay, deadlines: prios bucketed onto that day, busyMinutes}. Reuses calendar.ts and util.ts verbatim — no new data, no schema. Header carries a three-way LandingToggle (Week | Briefing | Board) extending the existing two-way toggle. Strength: closes the missing core surface most directly, visually obvious 'free vs packed', familiar timetable mental model students already know. Risk: a true positioned time-grid is the most pixel-fiddly of the three on narrow/mobile widths (columns get thin); mitigate with horizontal scroll-snap on the lane and a Mon-Fri default. Best when the team wants the unambiguous, demo-ready interpretation of the brief.

variants.name: Variant B — The Week Agenda (anxiety-first stacked list)
variants.approach: Reframe the week not as a spatial timetable but as a scannable to-do narrative — answers 'am I behind' first, 'when am I free' second. Vertical stack of 7 day-sections (Today, Tomorrow, then weekday names), each a compact card: a one-line narrated header reusing TodayWidget's SummaryLine logic per day ('Wed · 2 classes · 1 due · light'), then deadline chips (overdue/today/this-week buckets from priorities(), styled exactly like PrioritiesSection so it reads as one family), then the day's timed classes as simple time-prefixed rows (HH:MM Title · location, reusing EventsSection's row styling), and explicit 'No deadlines — free afternoon' empty states so free time is asserted in words, not whitespace. A per-day load pill (quiet/steady/heavy) sits in the header. Today's section is expanded by default; the rest are collapsible <details>-style so the whole week fits without horizontal scroll. Implementation: same buildWeek(ctx, now) selector as A, but rendered as a flex-column list of reused sub-components instead of a positioned grid — so it inherits BriefingView/TodayWidget styling almost for free and is far more robust on mobile/PWA (no time-axis math, no column thinning). Strength: lowest visual-risk, fastest to ship, mobile-perfect, and it keeps the emotional framing of the morning brief ('here is your week') which is the product's north star voice. It's effectively BriefingView x7. Risk: less visually striking than A's timetable; 'when am I free' is told rather than shown. Best when the team prioritises ship-speed, mobile parity, and tonal continuity with the existing Briefing over a bold new visual.

variants.name: Variant C — The Week Pulse (load-first heatmap + drill-down)
variants.approach: Bold, opinionated, single-glance answer to 'which days are brutal and where am I free' — invert the hierarchy so the per-day LOAD indicator IS the primary UI, not a footnote. Top of the view is a 7-cell week strip rendered as a sparkline/heatmap: each cell is a day whose height (or fill intensity) encodes that day's normalised load (busy class minutes + weighted deadline pressure from priorities()), so the whole week's shape — the two hellish days, the empty Thursday — is legible in under a second, the literal '90-second week'. Below the strip, the selected day (Today by default, click any cell to switch) expands into a single detailed column reusing Variant-A-style time blocks OR Variant-B-style agenda rows for just that day, so you get the macro pulse + micro detail without rendering all 7 detail panels at once. A thin overdue/at-risk banner spans the top ('3 things due before Friday') to hammer 'am I behind'. Implementation: same buildWeek(ctx, now) selector feeds a load[] array (max-normalised for the bar heights) plus the per-day detail; the heatmap strip is pure SVG/divs, the detail panel reuses existing widget sub-components. Cheapest to make responsive (the strip is 7 tiny cells, the detail is one column — no horizontal scroll, scales to mobile trivially) yet the most distinctive. Strength: most 'aggressive dogfood v2' — a genuinely new mental model (the week as a pressure waveform) that no calendar competitor leads with, and it makes the load indicator the hero instead of a decoration. Risk: it's one-day-at-a-time for detail, so it's less of a full-week-at-once timetable than A; the load heuristic needs a sensible weight (classes vs deadlines) or the heatmap misleads. Best when the team wants the high-conviction, differentiated bet that turns 'load' from a nice-to-have into the product's signature week view.

experiments.winner: Variant C — The Week Pulse (load-first heatmap + drill-down)

votes.preferred: Variant B — The Week Agenda (anxiety-first stacked list)
votes.why:
As Maya (first-year, lives on her phone), the question a "90-second week" has to answer is the same emotional one the morning brief already answers: "am I behind, and is this week going to crush me?" — not "what is my timetable," which I half-know anyway. Variant B answers that directly and is the one I'd actually open every day.

Concrete reasons:
- Tonal + trust continuity: B is literally BriefingView x7. It reuses SummaryLine ("Wed · 2 classes · 1 due · light") and the exact PrioritiesSection/EventsSection styling I already read and trust from TodayWidget.tsx. Deadlines (overdue/today/this-week buckets from priorities()) lead each day, which is my real anxiety — A and C lead with the timetable or a load bar instead. It keeps the calm "here is your week" voice that is the product's north star and the reason I use this over my school portal.
- Mobile/PWA reality: I'm on a ~380px screen. A's positioned 7-column time-grid means thin columns + horizontal scroll-snap — the fiddly thing I'd open once and abandon. B is a vertical flex-column of collapsible day cards, no time-axis math, no column thinning. It's mobile-perfect and the fastest to ship.
- Whole-week-at-once: the brief is about scanning everything in one pass. B lets me scroll the full week's actual deadlines and free-time in one go. C, despite being the coolest idea (week-as-pressure-waveform), is one-day-at-a-time for detail (click each cell) — that breaks the "see the week at a glance" promise for the part I care about most.
- Risk: B still gives the "which day is brutal" glance via a per-day load pill, but does NOT bet the entire UI on the load heuristic the way C does. C's heatmap misleads if the classes-vs-deadlines weight is off (2 classes reading heavier than one huge essay), and a misleading hero is worse than a footnote.
- Fit with prior rounds: Round 4's verified lesson (overnight-dogfood-2/round-4/feedback.md) was "deliver the outcome, don't make the user assemble it." B delivers the felt outcome (am I behind / where am I free, in words) with the least friction.

Implementation is real and self-contained: all of B's dependencies already exist — noteEvents/eventsOnDay (src/lib/calendar.ts), priorities()/deadlineEntries (src/components/widgets/util.ts via TodayWidget.tsx), and the reusable SummaryLine/PrioritiesSection/EventsSection sub-components in src/components/widgets/TodayWidget.tsx. A buildWeek(ctx, now) selector feeds reused sub-components; the three-way Week|Briefing|Board toggle extends the existing two-way toggle in src/components/sections/BriefingView.tsx. No new data, no schema, TypeScript stays green. C is the more exciting bet, but B is the one that survives contact with my actual Tuesday.

votes.preferred: Variant C — The Week Pulse (load-first heatmap + drill-down)
votes.why:
As Tom (freelancer), my week has shape, not a grid: a few scattered client calls, a couple of hard deadlines, and big stretches of unstructured work time. The question that actually matters to me is "which days this week will crush me, and where do I have real breathing room to take on more work or rest" — a load/pressure question, not a "where does my 10am sit on a time axis" question. Variant C makes load the hero: a 7-cell pulse strip answers "which two days are hell, where's the empty Thursday" in one glance, which IS the 90-second week for someone whose week isn't a rigid timetable, and the thin overdue/at-risk banner ("3 things due before Friday") nails "am I behind." Then I drill into the one day I care about.

Variant A is the wrong fit for me: it's built around a recurring Mon-Fri class timetable on an 08:00-20:00 axis (I confirmed it leans on noteEvents recurring expansion in calendar.ts). With 2-3 calls a day my grid would be 80% whitespace, and it's the most pixel-fiddly/fragile on narrow widths — I check this on my phone between meetings, so the horizontal-scroll thin columns are a dealbreaker. Variant B is genuinely safe, mobile-perfect, and tonally on-brand (it's literally BriefingView/TodayWidget's SummaryLine + PrioritiesSection styling x7, which I can see is a clean reuse), and I'd happily take it as the conservative ship — but stacking 7 day-cards means scrolling to compare days, and it TELLS me which day is heavy via a load pill instead of SHOWING the week's silhouette at once. That's the one thing I most want shown.

C also fits the constraints and the AGGRESSIVE dogfood-v2 mandate best: same buildWeek(ctx, now) selector over the existing priorities()/deadlineEntries()/eventsOnDay() data (no schema, no auth/payments/CI touched), the strip is pure divs/SVG so it's the cheapest of the three to make responsive (7 tiny cells + one reused detail column, no time-axis math, no column thinning), and it's a genuinely differentiated mental model rather than another calendar grid. The real risk — getting the classes-vs-deadlines load weight right so the heatmap doesn't mislead — is exactly the variable I care about most, and it's tunable; I'd accept owning that calibration to get the signature view.

votes.preferred: Variant C — The Week Pulse (load-first heatmap + drill-down)
votes.why:
As Sam (a power user who lives in this app and already gets the 90-second morning), C is the only variant that actually delivers a 90-second WEEK rather than a 90-second-day-times-seven. The brief is "extend the morning to the week," and the one question a week view must answer that the day view can't is "which days are brutal and where am I free" — a SHAPE question. C makes that shape the hero: a 7-cell load strip gives me the whole week's pressure waveform in one glance (the two hellish days, the empty Thursday), then I drill into one day for detail. A and B both bury load as a per-day footnote and force me to read 7 stacked panels to reconstruct the shape in my head — that's not a glance, that's homework.

My choice is also grounded in the codebase, which removes the usual reasons to retreat to the "safe" option:
- The data and selector layer are equally cheap for all three. noteEvents(calendarNotes, now) and eventsOnDay() in src/lib/calendar.ts already take a reference time and already expand recurring classes 30 days out; deadlineEntries()/priorities() in src/components/widgets/util.ts + TodayWidget.tsx already bucket deadlines. upcomingEvents in Dashboard.tsx (lines 886-892) is a 14-day window, so a full Mon-Sun week is comfortably covered. So buildWeek(ctx, now) producing a load[] array + per-day detail is the same lift whichever way it's rendered — there is no data advantage to picking A.
- C is the cheapest to make correct on mobile/PWA, which is a hard product constraint (shipped web PWA, primary user is students on phones). C's strip is 7 tiny divs and its detail is ONE column — no horizontal scroll, no time-axis math, no column-thinning. A's positioned 7-column time-grid is exactly the pixel-fiddly case that breaks on narrow widths (the variant text admits this and only mitigates with scroll-snap + a Mon-Fri default, i.e. it quietly drops to 5 days to survive).
- The reuse story actually favors C over B. priorities(), PrioritiesSection, EventsSection, SummaryLine and NextClassBanner are all PRIVATE to src/components/widgets/TodayWidget.tsx — none are exported. B's pitch ("BriefingView x7, inherits styling almost for free") therefore requires either exporting/refactoring the busiest widget file or duplicating ~150 lines of inline-styled JSX seven sections deep. C reuses those sub-components in a single detail panel, so it pays that extraction cost ONCE, not seven times. So B is not actually the lowest-effort option once you look at the source.

The brief explicitly frames this as dogfood loop v2 AGGRESSIVE — "favour bold, ambitious changes, not timid polish." A is the literal/safe read and B is openly the low-risk ship; both are fine products but neither is the bet this round is asking for. C is the high-conviction differentiated move: it turns "load" from a decoration into the product's signature week mental model (the week as a pressure waveform — no calendar competitor leads with that), while still being the most responsive and not materially more expensive to build. C's one real risk — being one-day-at-a-time for detail — is the right trade for a 90-second surface, because the whole point of a week glance is the macro shape, and I can drill in for any single day instantly. The one thing I'd insist on as the power user: get the load weight sane (busy class minutes + weighted deadline pressure, max-normalised) and expose at least overdue/at-risk in the top banner, because if the heatmap heuristic lies, the whole bet collapses.

experiments.priority: Module-grade tracking — add optional weight % + achieved mark to TopicItem and answer 'am I going to be okay this term'

variants.name: The Grade Ledger (per-module gradebook rollup)
variants.approach:
TREAT EACH TOPIC AS A MODULE GRADEBOOK. This is the literal reading of the brief and the safest, fastest bet.

Data (src/lib/types.ts:21-35, additive, same risk profile as the existing optional dueMin): add to TopicItem two optionals — `weight?: number | null` (percent of the module, 0-100) and `mark?: number | null` (achieved percent, 0-100). No migration; TS stays green because both are optional. Optionally add an opt-in `Topic.tracksGrades?: boolean` (or just auto-detect: a topic is 'graded' the moment any item has a weight) so non-academic topics (Girlfriend, Music) never sprout grade UI.

Capture (TopicTodosWidget.tsx, SortableTodoRow line 41): add a compact 'wt%' chip beside the existing DeadlineControl — click to type a weight; a second 'mark' field appears only after the item enters a done-stage (you don't have a mark until it's submitted+returned). Wire through the existing updateItem closure exactly like onDueMinChange (line 302). New TopicItems default both to null/undefined.

ROLLUP — the payoff. Add a `gradeRollup(topic)` helper (new src/lib/grades.ts) that computes: secured = sum(mark*weight) over marked items; lost = sum((100-mark)*weight); outstanding = sum(weight) of unmarked; weightDeclared = sum(weight). Then render a 'Module standing' card at the top of TopicDashboard (TopicView.tsx:44, alongside About/Links/Note — it already owns the topic header). The card shows a single horizontal stacked bar (secured green / lost red / still-to-play grey), the headline number ('62% secured of 70% graded so far'), and a projected-best/worst range ('final lands between 58% and 88%'). A `classify(pct)` map (>=70 First, 60-69 2:1, 50-59 2:2, 40-49 Third, else At risk) turns the projection into the emotional answer: 'On track for a 2:1 — you can afford to drop the 10% quiz.' Empty/zero-weight topics render nothing.

WHY DIFFERENT: this variant keeps all weight/mark intelligence INSIDE the module. The home/Today and Deadlines surfaces are untouched except for an optional small weight badge. It is the most encapsulated, lowest-blast-radius version: one new lib file + one card + two inline inputs. It answers 'am I okay in THIS module' precisely; the term-wide answer is implicit (open each module). Best if we want to ship the boldest-value feature with the least cross-surface churn and least chance of regressing the morning brief.

variants.name: The Term Survival Bar (cross-topic, weight-aware everything)
variants.approach:
MAKE WEIGHT A FIRST-CLASS SIGNAL THAT FLOWS EVERYWHERE — the same additive fields, but threaded through the shared deadline pipeline so the whole app gets weight-aware, and add a single term-wide answer surface.

Same TopicItem additions as variant 1 (weight?, mark?). The key architectural move: extend `DeadlineEntry` and the ListItem-projection in deadlineEntries (src/components/widgets/util.ts:50-100) to CARRY weight + mark (today it flattens TopicItem into a bare ListItem and DROPS everything that isn't a ListItem field — so weight would silently vanish without this). Add `weight?: number` and `mark?: number` to DeadlineEntry; copy them through in the topic-item loop. This single change is what lets every existing consumer (Today, DeadlinesView, UpcomingDeadlinesWidget) see weight for free.

VISIBLE HEFT (the brief's explicit ask):
- TodayWidget priorities(): change the sort tiebreak at TodayWidget.tsx:85 from pure due-time to weight-desc-within-bucket, so a 40%-of-module essay outranks a formative quiz due the same day. In PrioritiesSection (line 173) render a weight badge ('40%') and scale the left accent bar thickness/opacity by weight so heavy items literally look heavier.
- DeadlinesView rows (DeadlinesView.tsx:231): add the same '40%' badge and a heavier left border for high-weight items; add a 'Heaviest first' sort toggle next to the existing chronological grouping.

TERM ANSWER: add a slim, dismissible 'Term standing' banner to HomeView (above the grid) AND/OR a new lightweight 'Grades'/'This term' section that aggregates gradeRollup across ALL graded topics into one line: 'Across 5 modules: 64% secured, on track for a 2:1 — your heaviest unmarked item is the Stats coursework (40%, due Fri).' It names the single highest-leverage thing to do next.

WHY DIFFERENT: variant 1 answers 'am I okay in this module'; THIS answers 'am I okay THIS TERM' as the headline, and makes weight change behaviour app-wide (ranking, badges, heft) rather than living in one card. Bigger blast radius (touches util.ts, TodayWidget ranker, DeadlinesView, HomeView) but it is the boldness-5 version: it converts the whole knife, not just the topic page. Higher regression surface on the morning brief, so it needs care around the priorities() sort and the deadlineEntries projection.

variants.name: The Target & What-If Planner (interactive grade simulator)
variants.approach:
GO BEYOND TRACKING INTO PLANNING — same additive fields, but the centrepiece is an interactive 'what mark do I need?' simulator, which is the genuinely screenshot-worthy, no-competitor-builds-this angle.

Data: TopicItem gets weight? and mark? (as variants 1/2), PLUS `Topic.targetGrade?: number` (the mark the student is aiming for this module, e.g. 70 for a First). Still additive, still no migration.

CORE SURFACE — a 'Target' panel in TopicDashboard (TopicView.tsx:44). The student sets a target with a slider/preset chips (First / 2:1 / 2:2). The panel then computes, per UNMARKED item, the mark needed on the remaining weighted items to still hit target — the back-solve: needed_avg = (target*100 - securedPoints) / outstandingWeight. It renders the honest verdict: 'You need to average 68% across your 3 remaining assessments to land a 2:1' or, when impossible, 'A First is no longer reachable — 2:1 needs 55% from here' (the un-sugar-coated answer students actually want). Each remaining deadline row shows its personal 'need ≥X%' tag.

WHAT-IF: each marked item's mark is an inline editable number; dragging/typing it live-updates the projection and the classification — so a student can literally play 'if I get 75 on this essay, where do I land?' before they've submitted. This is the interactive, demo-able moment.

HOME TIE-IN (light): the highest-leverage unmarked item (highest weight * gap-to-target) gets a small 'do this first' marker in TodayWidget priorities — reusing the priorities() items already in hand at TodayWidget.tsx:70 — so the morning brief points at the assessment that most moves the needle, not just the nearest due date.

WHY DIFFERENT: variants 1 and 2 are descriptive ('here's where you stand'). THIS is prescriptive and forward-looking ('here's exactly what you must score, and what-if you experiment'). It is the most ambitious and the most novel — a grade calculator + planner fused into the topic page — and it directly answers the anxiety behind 'am I going to be okay' with an actionable number rather than a status bar. Implementation is self-contained to a new TargetPanel component + grades.ts math + one optional Today marker; heaviest design/logic lift of the three, lightest cross-surface churn after that.

experiments.winner: The Target & What-If Planner (interactive grade simulator)

votes.preferred: The Target & What-If Planner (interactive grade simulator)
votes.why:
As Maya, a first-year, the question "am I going to be okay this term" is not really a request for a status bar, it is anxiety asking for a number I can act on. Variant 1 (Grade Ledger) and variant 2 (Term Survival Bar) both tell me where I stand right now, but as a fresher I don't have many marks back yet, so a "62% secured of 70% graded" rollup is mostly empty and the bar just shows a lot of grey "still to play". That doesn't calm me. What I actually do at 11pm is the thing variant 3 builds: "if I get X on the stats coursework, can I still get a 2:1?" The back-solve ("you need to average 68% across your 3 remaining assessments") and the honest "a First is no longer reachable" answer is exactly the un-sugar-coated thing I want, and the live what-if where I drag a hypothetical mark and watch the classification move is the one moment I'd actually screenshot and send to my course group chat. That word-of-mouth pull matters for a dogfood-v2 bold bet.

On the realities I checked: all three share the same safe additive fields (TopicItem.weight?/mark? mirror the existing optional dueMin at types.ts:32, so TS stays green, no migration). Variant 3 stays self-contained to a new TargetPanel + grades.ts + one optional Today marker, which means it does NOT have to touch the fragile shared pipeline. Variant 2's core move is to thread weight through deadlineEntries (util.ts:50, which today flattens TopicItem and drops everything that isn't a ListItem field, line 64) AND re-sort priorities() at TodayWidget.tsx:85 — that is a direct rewrite of the morning-brief ranker, the app's north-star surface ("your morning in 90 seconds"), so it carries the highest regression risk of regressing the one thing the product can't break. As Maya I'd rather the morning brief stay rock-solid and have the powerful planner live on the topic page where I go deliberately when I'm stressed about a module. Variant 1 is the safest but it's descriptive only and answers "this module" not "this term," and an empty ledger for a first-year is underwhelming. Variant 3 gives the boldest, genuinely novel value (a grade calculator + planner no competitor bundles into a life dashboard) while keeping cross-surface churn light — the best risk/payoff trade for me.

votes.preferred: The Target & What-If Planner (interactive grade simulator)
votes.why:
As Tom, the "am I going to be okay" question is an anxiety question, and what actually answers anxiety is a number I can act on, not a status bar that just describes where I stand. Variants 1 and 2 are descriptive ("here's where you are"); only variant 3 is prescriptive ("you need to average 68% across your 3 remaining assessments to land a 2:1" / "a First is no longer reachable"). That back-solve maps onto how I actually think as a freelancer: what do I need to hit from here to be fine. The what-if slider (drag a projected mark, watch the classification update live before I've even submitted) is the genuinely novel, screenshot-worthy moment none of the alternatives have, and it's the most directly reassuring.

Crucially it also respects the project's hard constraint better than variant 2. The north star is "your morning in 90 seconds," and variant 2 rewires the two most fragile pieces of that brief: the priorities() sort tiebreak (TodayWidget.tsx:85) and the deadlineEntries projection (util.ts:50-100, which today deliberately flattens TopicItem to a bare ListItem). Re-ranking my morning by weight is exactly the kind of surprising regression I'd resent. As a freelancer I don't have 5 modules, so variant 2's cross-topic term banner is mostly dead weight for me anyway, and variant 1's per-module bar makes me open each topic and aggregate "this term" in my own head, which is the very thing the brief says to answer.
