Variant 3 keeps the heavy logic in a self-contained TargetPanel + grades.ts, and its Today tie-in is a small "do this first" marker reusing the priorities() items already in hand (TodayWidget.tsx:70), a non-destructive additive read rather than a behavior-changing re-sort. Data is the same additive optionals (weight?, mark?, plus Topic.targetGrade?) so TypeScript stays green with no migration. Bold and novel where it counts, conservative where the morning brief lives. The honest, un-sugar-coated verdict ("a First is no longer reachable, 2:1 needs 55% from here") is the answer I'd actually trust.

votes.preferred: The Target & What-If Planner (interactive grade simulator)
votes.why:
As Sam, a power user, the question in the brief is literally an anxiety question — "am I going to be okay this term" — and the only variant that answers it the way I actually think is the Planner. The other two tell me where I stand; I already roughly know where I stand. What I do not know, and what I lie awake over, is the actionable number: "to still get my 2:1, what do I have to score on the three things I have not handed in yet?" That back-solve (needed_avg = (target*100 - securedPoints) / outstandingWeight) is the whole point. A status bar (variant 1) or a term banner (variant 2) leaves me to do that arithmetic in my head at 1am, which is exactly the chore I want the tool to kill.

The what-if interaction is the part I would use daily and the part that makes me show it to my coursemates: typing "if I get 75 on this essay" and watching the projected classification flip is the demo-able, no-competitor-has-this moment the brief is fishing for ("screenshot-worthy"). Descriptive tracking is commodity; a what-if simulator fused into the topic page is not. For a dogfood loop explicitly told to favour bold, ambitious changes over timid polish, this is the boldest in user-value terms even though variant 2 is bolder in blast radius.

On risk, which I care about because I do not want my morning brief regressing: I verified the load-bearing claims. deadlineEntries in src/components/widgets/util.ts:63-67 really does flatten TopicItem into a bare ListItem and drop anything that is not a ListItem field, so variant 2's "thread weight through the shared pipeline" genuinely means surgery on the one projection that feeds Today, DeadlinesView, and UpcomingDeadlines plus changing the priorities() sort at TodayWidget.tsx:85 — that is real regression surface on the 90-second brief, the app's north star, for a payoff (heavier-looking rows, a dismissible banner) that is mostly cosmetic. The Planner, by contrast, is self-contained: a new TargetPanel in TopicDashboard (confirmed it owns the topic header at src/components/sections/TopicView.tsx:44), the grades.ts math, and one optional non-structural "do this first" marker that reuses the priorities() items already in hand at TodayWidget.tsx:70 without touching the sort. Same additive, no-migration data shape as the others (weight?/mark? on TopicItem alongside the existing optional dueMin at types.ts:32, plus Topic.targetGrade?), so TS stays green and non-academic topics like Girlfriend/Music never sprout grade UI.

Variant 1 is the safe minimum and I would happily take it if forced, but it under-delivers on the brief's own ambition and still makes me open every module to get the term picture. Variant 2 spends its risk budget on app-wide cosmetics and a banner rather than on the answer I actually want, and it is the one most likely to break the morning brief. The Planner gives me the prescriptive, forward-looking answer ("you need to average 68% from here", or the honest "a First is no longer reachable") with the lightest cross-surface churn after its self-contained logic lift. That is the right trade for an aggressive loop: maximum novel user value, contained to one page.

experiments.priority: Free, local, deadline-aware notifications — make the 90-second morning reach the user before they open the app

variants.name: Mirror the email watcher: a parallel local deadline watcher (briefs + per-deadline nudges)
variants.approach: Build the deadline notifier as a structural twin of the existing alerts.ts email watcher, so it reuses the proven loop/state/notify scaffolding but stays a separate, free, local concern. NEW FILE src/lib/deadlineNudge.ts that mirrors alerts.ts: a pure scheduling core plus a poll loop. Extract the deadline math so it is shared, not duplicated — lift the `priorities()` bucketing out of TodayWidget.tsx (lines 62-86) into a pure function `computePriorities(ctx, todayMs)` in src/components/widgets/util.ts beside the existing `deadlineEntries`/`dueTimestamp`, then call it from both the widget (no behavior change) and the watcher. deadlineNudge.ts then computes two notification kinds from that one source: (1) a once-per-day MORNING DIGEST fired at a user-set time (default 08:00) — title 'Your morning in 90 seconds', body assembled exactly like TodayWidget's SummaryLine ('1 overdue · 2 due today · next class 14:00') from overdue/dueToday counts + nextEvent; (2) PER-DEADLINE NUDGES at fixed offsets before each item's `dueTimestamp` (e.g. 24h / 1h, configurable), title = topic/section label, body = item text + 'due in 1h'. Dedupe with the same ring-buffer pattern alerts.ts already uses (loadWatchState/saveWatchState/capRing) but under a separate STATE_KEY ('deadlineNudgeState') keyed by a stable string like `${itemId}@${offset}` and a `lastDigestDay` field so the digest fires once per calendar day. Notification delivery reuses alerts.ts's exact pattern: dynamic `import('@tauri-apps/plugin-notification')` with the existing `ensurePermission()` gate (export it from alerts.ts so it is shared, not re-implemented), and ADD a web fallback inside notify — when `isWeb()`, use the browser `Notification` API (request permission once, `new Notification(title,{body})`) so the PWA also gets nudges. Wire-up mirrors the email watcher in Dashboard.tsx: a sibling useEffect that calls a new `startDeadlineWatcher({ ctx-data, settings })` / `stopDeadlineWatcher()`, re-running when topics/upcomingEvents/dailyPlan/settings change; poll every ~60s (cheap — pure in-memory recompute, no network) so digest-time and offset crossings are caught even on a long-running tray session. CRITICAL the watcher is gated only by a free local toggle (new NotifySettings: { digestEnabled, digestHour, nudgeOffsetsHrs[] }) stored in synced storage like priorityAlerts — NO Plus check anywhere, unlike the email watcher. Pros: lowest-risk (clones a shipped, trusted pattern), clean separation of the free promise from the Plus upsell, dedupe/quiet-hours logic already battle-tested. This is the conservative, ship-it-tonight option.

variants.name: Fold deadlines into a single unified notification engine (one loop, pluggable sources)
variants.approach: Rather than a second parallel watcher, REFACTOR alerts.ts into one generic notification engine that pulls from multiple 'sources', of which email-priority is one (Plus-gated) and deadline-nudge is another (free, local). Introduce a `NotificationSource` shape: `{ id, isEnabled(), collect(): Promise<PendingNotif[]>, gate?: 'plus' | 'free' }` where PendingNotif = `{ key: string, title, body, fireAt?: number, onFire?() }`. The existing email logic becomes `emailSource` (gate:'plus', collect() = current syncAllAccounts + matchRules mapping each caught message to a PendingNotif keyed by msg.id). A new `deadlineSource` (gate:'free') reuses the lifted `computePriorities` (extracted from TodayWidget.tsx:70 into util.ts) to emit: a daily-digest PendingNotif keyed `digest@<dayMs>` whose `fireAt` is today's digest hour, plus per-item PendingNotifs keyed `<itemId>@<offsetHrs>` whose `fireAt = dueTimestamp(item) - offset`. The single `runNotificationCheck()` loop: for each enabled source, collect() its pending notifs, drop any whose key is in the shared notifiedIds ring buffer, drop any with `fireAt > now` (not due yet) or `fireAt` far in the past beyond a grace window, respect the existing inQuietHours() (which now protects ALL notifications), fire via a shared notify() that branches Tauri-plugin vs web `Notification`, then persist keys. One `startWatcher()`/`stopWatcher()` + one setInterval (~60s) replaces the email-only one; Dashboard.tsx's single watcher useEffect now feeds it both the oauthAccounts (for emailSource) and the live ctx deadline data (for deadlineSource), and the engine internally skips plus-gated sources when entitlement is absent while ALWAYS running free ones. Pros: no duplicated loop/dedupe/quiet-hours code, deadlines and email share one permission prompt and one consistent notification voice, and future notifiers (habit reminders, pomodoro) drop in as new sources for free. The deadline nudge can never accidentally be Plus-gated because `gate:'free'` sources bypass the entitlement check by construction. This is the architecturally cleanest, most extensible option but touches the shipped email path so it carries refactor risk; mitigate by keeping emailSource behavior byte-for-byte and adding a focused test around the gate logic so the Plus email path stays exactly as before.

variants.name: Scheduled morning digest via the OS scheduler with an in-app inline-nudge fallback
variants.approach: Treat the two halves of the promise differently to maximize reliability of the headline 'morning brief reaches you before you open the app'. HALF A — the morning digest — does NOT rely on the app being awake at 08:00. Snapshot the morning brief text whenever the app is running (recompute `computePriorities` lifted from TodayWidget into util.ts on each topics/events change, debounced) and persist a tiny `pendingDigest = { text, forDayMs }` to storage. On Tauri, register a real OS-level scheduled notification for the next digest time using the notification plugin's schedule capability (the plugin supports a `schedule` option on sendNotification, or fall back to spawning the notification when the tray app's interval crosses the digest hour) so the digest can fire even if the JS poll loop missed the exact minute; the body is the last-snapshotted brief, so it is accurate as of the last time Bozz saw the data. On web/PWA where background scheduling is unavailable, degrade gracefully: show the digest as a prominent in-app 'good morning' banner on first load of the day (a new lightweight component above the home grid, reading pendingDigest) AND fire an immediate `Notification` if the tab is open — honest about platform limits rather than pretending. HALF B — per-deadline nudges — runs as a lightweight in-process scheduler: when the app is open it schedules `setTimeout`s for each upcoming deadline's near-term offsets (e.g. anything within the next 6h gets an exact-fire timeout; longer-horizon ones are re-evaluated by a 5-min sweep), firing OS/web notifications at the precise `dueTimestamp - offset` rather than polling on a coarse interval, which gives minute-accurate 'due in 1h' nudges and avoids waking on a 60s loop. State/dedupe still uses the alerts.ts ring-buffer pattern under a new key so a nudge never double-fires across reloads. Delivery shares one notify() helper (Tauri plugin when isTauri(), browser Notification when isWeb()) and one permission prompt. All of it is free + local, no Plus gate, no server. Pros: most faithful to 'reach the user before they open the app' because the digest does not depend on the poll loop being alive at the right second, exact-time nudges feel premium, and the web path is handled with an honest in-app fallback instead of silently doing nothing. Cons/risk: depends on the notification plugin's scheduling surface (verify capability; if scheduling is unavailable, the interval-crossing fallback still works), and the per-deadline setTimeout scheduler needs care to clear/reschedule cleanly on data changes to avoid leaks — but it is the variant that best honors the literal north-star wording.

experiments.winner: Mirror the email watcher: a parallel local deadline watcher (briefs + per-deadline nudges)

votes.preferred: Scheduled morning digest via the OS scheduler with an in-app inline-nudge fallback
votes.why: As Maya (first-year, mostly on a laptop that's shut overnight and often on the web PWA), I judge these purely by whether the brief actually reaches me before I open Bozz, and only variant 3 honestly delivers that. I checked the real code: alerts.ts fires the digest off a coarse setInterval poll (startAlertWatcher, ~60s) and notify()/ensurePermission() are hard Tauri-only (return false on web). So variants 1 and 2 both inherit the same fatal weakness for ME: the 08:00 digest only fires if the app happened to be awake at exactly that minute. I open my laptop at 8:05, the loop wasn't alive at 8:00, and I get nothing, which is the exact moment the north-star promise breaks and I stop trusting it. Variant 3 snapshots the brief (computePriorities lifted from TodayWidget.tsx:70 into util.ts) and registers a real OS-level scheduled notification, so the digest can fire even when the poll loop missed the minute. That's the difference between a feature that works once and one I rely on. Second, it's the only variant that takes my platform seriously: on web/PWA where background scheduling doesn't exist it degrades honestly with a prominent good-morning banner on first load of the day plus an immediate Notification if the tab's open, instead of silently pretending. I'd rather be told 'this part is laptop-app-only' than have it quietly no-op. Third, the per-deadline setTimeout scheduler gives minute-accurate 'due in 1h' nudges rather than a notification that lands whenever the 60s loop next wakes, which makes the nudges feel precise instead of laggy. All of it is explicitly free + local with no Plus gate, which matters to me as a broke student. Variant 1 is just a safe clone of the same coarse-poll weakness, and variant 2's clean unified engine is real engineering elegance I as a user never see, while it refactors the shipped Plus email path (the constraints warn against touching payments/auth-adjacent code) for a benefit that doesn't reach me. Variant 3's stated risk is verifying the notification plugin's schedule surface, but it already names an interval-crossing fallback if scheduling is unavailable, so the downside is bounded and it still beats the others. It's the only one that actually honors 'reach the user before they open the app.'

votes.preferred: Mirror the email watcher: a parallel local deadline watcher (briefs + per-deadline nudges)
votes.why:
As Tom (freelancer), I care about one thing: a reliable deadline brief that lands before I open the app, tonight, without breaking the email alerts I already trust. Variant 1 gives me exactly that with the least risk.

Why not variant 3 (OS-scheduler), even though its pitch sounds the most faithful to the north star: I checked the actual project and its headline benefit is not real here. The granted Tauri capability is only `notification:default` (permission + send); there is no `notification:allow-schedule`/`cancel`/`pending`, and the v2 plugin's `schedule` option is effectively mobile-only and ignored by the desktop backend anyway. Making it work means editing `src-tauri` capabilities, which is a HARD CONSTRAINT violation (never touch CI/release/Tauri config beyond frontend). So variant 3's whole premise collapses to its own fallback (interval-crossing) plus a setTimeout scheduler that needs careful clear/reschedule on every data change to avoid leaks and double-fires. That is more moving parts for a benefit I won't actually get. The honest in-app good-morning banner is nice but it's a different feature, and per the codebase the tray already stays alive via prevent_close (lib.rs:460), so a plain 60s poll already catches the 08:00 crossing on desktop, which is my real use case.

Why not variant 2 (unified engine): architecturally it's the prettiest and I get the appeal of one loop and 'gate:free can never be Plus-gated by construction.' But it refactors the SHIPPED, Plus-monetised email path that already works and that I rely on. The constraint is to keep TypeScript green and not touch payments/entitlement, and folding the email watcher into a generic source layer puts the one revenue feature one regression away from breaking for an extensibility benefit (habit/pomodoro notifiers) that isn't being asked for tonight. That's a bad trade in an aggressive dogfood round where I want a bold-but-safe shipped win, not a risky rewrite.

Variant 1 is the sweet spot: it clones a trusted pattern (loadWatchState/saveWatchState/capRing, inQuietHours, ensurePermission, the Dashboard sibling-useEffect wiring), shares the deadline math by lifting `priorities()` into the already-pure util.ts (no behavior change to TodayWidget), and crucially keeps the free promise structurally separate from the Plus upsell with NO Plus check — exactly the right boundary for the 'free, local' goal. The web fallback via the existing `isWeb()` + browser Notification means my PWA also gets nudges. It's self-contained, frontend-only, leaves the email path byte-for-byte untouched, and ships tonight. That reliably delivers my 90-second morning before I open the app. The only mild downside, a little structural duplication of the loop, is cheap and can be unified later if more notifiers actually arrive.

votes.preferred: Mirror the email watcher: a parallel local deadline watcher (briefs + per-deadline nudges)
votes.why:
As Sam (power user) I want this shipped tonight, reliable, and provably free. Variant A clones a pattern I already trust in production (alerts.ts: loadWatchState/saveWatchState/capRing/inQuietHours ring-buffer dedupe) into a separate deadlineNudge.ts, so the deadline feature can't regress my Plus email watcher. I verified that file is self-contained and its scaffolding lifts cleanly; the only shared math to extract is priorities() (TodayWidget.tsx:70-86), which already sits on deadlineEntries/dueTimestamp in util.ts, so computePriorities is a low-risk lift with no widget behavior change.

The decisive factor is the hard requirement that deadline nudges are NEVER Plus-gated. The email watcher's gate lives behind priorityAlerts.enabled + isPlus()/BETA_UNLOCK, separate from this useEffect. Variant A keeps that separation structural: a brand-new free NotifySettings toggle with no isPlus() reference anywhere, gated only by a local flag. That's the safest guarantee the free promise can't accidentally inherit a paywall.

I rejected B (unified engine) despite it being architecturally cleaner: it refactors the SHIPPED email path, and as the actual Plus user I'm the one who eats a regression if the gate logic or quiet-hours behavior shifts even slightly. A 'gate:free bypasses entitlement by construction' design is elegant but I don't want my paid notifications and my free notifications sharing one mutable loop the night it ships. The extensibility (habit/pomodoro sources) is real but premature for round 1.

I rejected C (OS scheduler + setTimeout) even though I confirmed @tauri-apps/plugin-notification@2.3.3 really does expose schedule?: Schedule (ScheduleEvery/ScheduleInterval) so its core claim is true. It's the most faithful to 'reach me before I open the app,' but it's the highest-variance option: a per-deadline setTimeout scheduler that must clear/reschedule on every topics/events change is exactly the kind of thing that leaks timers or double-fires across reloads, and the snapshot-pendingDigest dance plus a web banner fallback is a lot of new surface for one night. For a tray app that lib.rs already keeps alive in the background, A's 60s in-memory recompute is cheap and catches digest-time and offset crossings reliably enough — I'd take exact-minute nudges as a fast-follow once the watcher is proven.

One concrete improvement I'd insist on inside variant A as written: it already calls for adding the web Notification fallback inside notify() and exporting ensurePermission() from alerts.ts so it's shared. Today notify()/ensurePermission() are Tauri-only and email-specific, so that web branch is mandatory for the PWA to be reached at all. That keeps A honest about the north star while staying the conservative, ship-it-tonight choice.

changes.title: Add the 'week' home landing to the type + appearance plumbing (back-compat)
changes.approach: Widen the homeLanding union at types.ts:404 from 'briefing' | 'board' to 'briefing' | 'board' | 'week' (undefined still falls back to 'board', exactly as today). In Dashboard.tsx widen the local type + setter at lines 707-708 (homeLanding/setHomeLanding) to include 'week'. This is the only schema-shaped change and it is additive/optional, so TS stays green and existing saved 'briefing'/'board' values are untouched. In SettingsView.tsx:491-494 add a third Segmented option { id: 'week', label: 'This week' } so users can pick it. Leave the brand-new-account default at 'briefing' (Dashboard.tsx:312-313) for now so we do not destabilise onboarding; the Week view is opt-in this round and reachable from the in-view toggle (next change).
changes.risk: Low. Pure additive union widening mirroring how 'briefing' was added; no migration, no persisted-value breakage. Only watch item: any exhaustive switch on homeLanding (none found beyond the two equality checks in Dashboard.tsx) would need the new arm.

changes.title: Build WeekView — Variant C 'Week Pulse' (load heatmap + day drill-down) as the third landing
changes.approach: New WeekView.tsx, pure composition over WidgetCtx exactly like BriefingView. Compute the current Mon-Sun (with a Mon-Fri/Mon-Sun toggle) from new Date(). For each day: classes/events via eventsOnDay(noteEvents(ctx.calendarNotes) + feed events already in ctx.upcomingEvents, day); deadlines for that day by filtering deadlineEntries(ctx)/dueTimestamp(util.ts) onto the day; planned tasks from ctx.dailyPlan[dayKey]. Derive a per-day load score = weighted sum of timed-event hours + deadline count (boosted by weight%, see change 4) and render the 7 days as a heatmap row of intensity-shaded cells (use t.doingAccent at varying alpha; a clear/empty cell when load is 0 so free days read as visibly empty). Clicking a day expands a drill-down panel below reusing the same event/deadline/task row styling as TodayWidget's sub-sections (lift those row renderers or copy the compact markup). Today's column gets a ring/accent border. Mount it in Dashboard.tsx alongside the briefing/board branches (around lines 1570-1617): add activeSection==='home' && homeLanding==='week' rendering WeekView with the same ctx object already built for BriefingView, passing onSwitchToBoard/onSwitchToBriefing. Extend the Briefing/Board toggle in BriefingView.tsx (and the mirror in HomeView.tsx:679) to a 3-way Briefing/Week/Board so users can reach it. Keep maxWidth ~760px to match the brief. No new ctx fields needed — todayEvents is today-only, so WeekView leans on upcomingEvents (14-day, already provided) + noteEvents recomputed for the week window.
changes.risk: Medium. Largest surface but zero schema/auth/sync risk — it only reads existing computed state. Main risks are visual (heatmap intensity scaling, empty-week emptiness) and that upcomingEvents is a 14-day list so a Mon-Sun window is fully covered; recurring classes already expand 30 days via noteEvents so they are present. Guard against undefined ctx.calendarNotes/upcomingEvents (all optional in WidgetCtx).

changes.title: Add optional weight% + achieved mark to TopicItem and the precise-due/priority plumbing
changes.approach: Add two optional fields to TopicItem (types.ts:21-35): weight?: number | null (percent of module, 0-100) and achieved?: number | null (mark scored, percent). Same additive/optional profile as the existing dueMin field — no migration, TS green. Surface editing where the deadline is already edited: extend DeadlineControl.tsx (or add a tiny sibling control rendered next to it in TopicTodosWidget.tsx:136) with two compact numeric inputs 'weight %' and 'mark %', persisted via onTopicChange the same way onDueMinChange already updates the item. In util.ts carry weight/achieved through the DeadlineEntry.item projection (deadlineEntries builds a ListItem at lines 63-66) so downstream views can read them. Imported calendar deadlines simply have weight undefined (unchanged).
changes.risk: Low. Optional fields, no migration. ListItem type may need the two optional fields added so the util.ts projection type-checks (verify ListItem definition). Editing UI is the only fiddly part — keep inputs validated 0-100 and store null when blank.

changes.title: Module rollup + Target & What-If grade simulator as a new topic-page widget
changes.approach: The topic page is a widget grid (TopicView.tsx:284), so deliver this as a topic-only widget. New ModuleGradeWidget.tsx reads ctx.topics + ctx.currentTopicId. Compute the rollup from that topic's items: secured% = sum(weight_i * achieved_i/100) over graded items; remaining weight = 100 - sum(weight) of all weighted items. Render the 'X% secured / Y% still to play for' bar and a classification hint (>=70 First, >=60 2:1, >=50 2:2, >=40 Pass) for the projected total. Then the What-If simulator: a single 'target final grade' slider/number; given secured + remaining weight, compute and display 'you need an average of N% across your remaining N assessments to hit that target' (and flag impossible targets in t.alert). All pure arithmetic, recomputed on the fly, no persistence beyond the weight/achieved fields from change 3. Register it in registry.tsx WIDGET_REGISTRY as type 'moduleGrade' (add to the WidgetType union in types.ts:344-350), include in TOPIC_WIDGET_LIST, and exclude from HOME via HIDDEN_FROM_HOME_PANEL. Do NOT auto-add it to DEFAULT_TOPIC_LAYOUT this round (opt-in via Add Widget) to avoid disturbing existing topic layouts.
changes.risk: Medium. Self-contained but the classification thresholds are UK-degree-specific; keep them as plain labels and avoid over-claiming. Adding a WidgetType member means every exhaustive map over WidgetType (WIDGET_REGISTRY is the main one) must include it or TS fails — that is the gate that keeps it safe.

changes.title: Give high-weight deadlines visible heft in Deadlines + Today priority rows
changes.approach: With weight now on DeadlineEntry.item (change 3), surface it. In TodayWidget.tsx PrioritiesSection (lines 194-227) render a small '40%' badge when p.item.weight is set and give weight>=25 rows a heavier left accent (thicker borderLeft / bolder text). Optionally bias the priorities() sort (lines 84-85) so that within the same bucket+day, higher weight ranks first (tie-break only — never reorder across overdue/today/week). In DeadlinesView.tsx item rows (lines 235-266) add the same % badge next to the urgency label and thicken the accent bar for high-weight items. Purely presentational reads of the new optional field; items without weight render exactly as today.
changes.risk: Low. Reads optional fields with fallbacks; existing rows unchanged when weight is undefined. Only behavioural change is an intra-bucket sort tie-break, which is safe and reversible.

changes.title: Free local deadline watcher — parallel to the email watcher (morning brief + per-deadline nudges)
changes.approach: New deadlineAlerts.ts mirroring alerts.ts structure but with NO Plus gate and NO server/email dependency. Reuse the same notification path: a notify() that does the isTauri()/@tauri-apps/plugin-notification import (copy ensurePermission/notify from alerts.ts:99-131) AND a web Notification fallback for the PWA (if !isTauri && 'Notification' in window, request permission + new Notification). Provide startDeadlineWatcher/stopDeadlineWatcher with a setInterval loop like startAlertWatcher (alerts.ts:194-203). The check runs the SAME computation the UI uses: pass it the deadlineEntries-equivalent input (topics + upcomingEvents) and compute dueTimestamp per item; fire (a) a once-per-day morning digest ('3 things today, next class 14:00') built from the priorities() buckets, and (b) per-deadline nudges at coarse thresholds (e.g. due-today and ~1h-before for timed dueMin items). Track fired ids + lastDigestDay in a local-only storage key via getItem/setItem (mirror loadWatchState/saveWatchState, never synced) so nothing double-fires. Wire it in Dashboard.tsx with a new useEffect next to the email-watcher effect (lines 487-519): start it whenever !loading and topics/upcomingEvents exist; restart on those deps; always stop on cleanup. Add a single free toggle ('Deadline reminders') in PriorityAlertsBlock.tsx, persisted in a small local/synced setting separate from the Plus email rules.
changes.risk: Medium. No auth/payment/secret touched and no Supabase/CI changes. Real risks are notification spam (mitigated by per-id de-dupe + once-per-day digest in local-only state) and permission UX on web (gate behind an explicit user opt-in toggle, request permission only on enable). Keep it strictly free/local per the brief — do not import anything from the email/Plus path.

plan.summary: Six grounded, additive changes deliver all three P1 bets without touching auth/payments/secrets/Supabase/CI. (1+2) Add a third homeLanding='week' (union-widened exactly like 'briefing' was at types.ts:404, consumed in Dashboard.tsx:707) and a new WeekView.tsx implementing the user-chosen Variant C 'Week Pulse' — a 7-day load heatmap with day drill-down, pure composition over WidgetCtx using the already-computed noteEvents()/eventsOnDay() (calendar.ts), deadlineEntries()/dueTimestamp() (util.ts) and dailyPlan, mirroring the low-risk BriefingView pattern. (3+4+5) Add optional weight%/achieved fields to TopicItem (same risk profile as the existing optional dueMin field — no migration), edited beside the deadline in TopicTodosWidget, rolled up by a new ModuleGradeWidget topic widget that ships the chosen 'Target & What-If Planner' grade simulator, and surfaced as % badges + heavier accents in TodayWidget priorities and DeadlinesView. (6) A free, local deadlineAlerts.ts watcher mirroring the email watcher's structure and reusing its Tauri notification path (plus a web Notification fallback) to fire a once-per-day morning digest and per-deadline nudges off the same priorities computation, gated only by a free opt-in toggle — explicitly no Plus gate, no server. Key gotchas: adding a WidgetType member forces the new arm in WIDGET_REGISTRY (TS gate), ListItem likely needs the two optional fields for util.ts to type-check, and the deadline watcher must de-dupe via a local-only storage key to avoid notification spam.
